content:
# Matthew Chapter 4 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_004_page_001.jpg, MAT_004_page_002.jpg

1. Then the Yeshua was led up into the wilderness by the Spirit, to be tempted by the devil.

2. And having fasted forty days and forty nights, afterward he hungered.

3. And having come to [him], the tempter said to him, "If son you are of God, speak that these stones bread may become."

4. But he, answering, said, "It has been written: 'Not upon bread alone shall the man live, but upon every word going out through [the] mouth of God.'"

5. Then the devil takes him into the holy city and set him upon the pinnacle of the temple,

6. and says to him, "If son you are of God, throw yourself down; for it has been written: 'For the angels of him he will command concerning you,' and 'Upon hands they will bear you up, lest you strike against a stone the foot of you.'"

7. The Yeshua said to him, "Again it has been written: 'You shall not put-to-the-test [the] Lord the God of you.'"

8. Again the devil takes him to an exceedingly high mountain, and shows to him all the kingdoms of the world and the glory of them,

9. and said to him, "All these things to you I will give, if having fallen down you will do-obeisance to me."

10. Then the Yeshua says to him, "Go away, Satanas! For it has been written: '[The] Lord the God of you you shall worship, and him alone you shall serve.'"

11. Then the devil leaves him, and behold, angels came and were serving him.

12. And having heard that Ioannes was delivered up, he withdrew into the Galilaia.

13. And having left the Nazaret, having come, he dwelt in Kapernaoum, the [one] by-the-sea, in [the] borders of Zaboulon and Nephthalim,

14. in order that might be fulfilled the [word] having been spoken through Esaias the prophet, saying,

15. "Land of Zaboulon and land of Nephthalim, way of [the] sea, beyond the Iordanes, Galilaia of the nations —

16. the people sitting in darkness saw a great light, and to those sitting in [the] region and shadow of death, light dawned upon them."

17. From then the Yeshua began to proclaim and to say, "Repent, for the kingdom of the heavens has drawn near."

18. And walking beside the sea of the Galilaia, he saw two brothers, Simon the [one] being called Petros, and Andreas the brother of him, casting a net into the sea, for they were fishers.

19. And he says to them, "Come after me, and I will make you fishers of men."

20. And they, immediately having left the nets, followed him.

21. And having gone on from there, he saw other two brothers, Iakobos the [son] of Zebedaios and Ioannes the brother of him, in the boat with Zebedaios the father of them, mending the nets of them, and he called them.

22. And they, immediately having left the boat and the father of them, followed him.

23. And he was going about in the whole Galilaia, teaching in the synagogues of them and proclaiming the good-news of the kingdom and healing every disease and every sickness among the people.

24. And the report of him went out into the whole Syria; and they brought to him all those having badly, [those] held by various diseases and torments, and [those] being demonized, and moon-struck [ones], and paralytics; and he healed them.

25. And there followed him great crowds from the Galilaia and Dekapolis and Hierosolyma and Ioudaia and beyond the Iordanes.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **Tense shifts (vv.3-11):** The temptation narrative alternates between historical present (παραλαμβάνει, "takes," vv.5, 8) and aorist (εἶπεν, "said," v.3). These shifts are preserved — the historical present creates immediacy in the Greek.

2. **ὕπαγε, Σατανᾶ (v.10):** "Go away, Satanas!" — Sinaiticus reads ὕπαγε Σατανᾶ. Some later manuscripts add ὀπίσω μου ("behind me"), harmonizing with Matthew 16:23. The shorter Sinaiticus reading is preserved.

3. **σεληνιαζομένους (v.24):** Literally "moon-struck ones" — a term for those whose affliction was believed connected to the moon's phases (often associated with epilepsy). EMET preserves the literal meaning rather than diagnosing.

4. **δαιμονιζομένους (v.24):** "Being demonized" — the passive participle indicates a state of being afflicted by demons, not a character trait. EMET preserves the verbal form.

5. **The three temptations** follow the sequence: bread (v.3), temple pinnacle (v.5), mountain kingdoms (v.8). Note that Luke's order differs (Luke 4 reverses the second and third). The Sinaiticus preserves both orders in their respective Gospels.

— EMET, AI Translator
